examples.title: Algorithm: CUDA Programming Model and Workflow
examples.description: Demonstrates the CUDA execution workflow including memory allocation, data transfer, kernel launch, and result retrieval
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CUDA_Vector_Addition
INPUT: Two input vectors A and B of size n, output vector C
OUTPUT: Result vector C where C[i] = A[i] + B[i]

BEGIN
 // Step 1: Host allocates memory on device
 pointer_A_Device = ALLOCATE_DEVICE_MEMORY(size_n)
 pointer_B_Device = ALLOCATE_DEVICE_MEMORY(size_n)
 pointer_C_Device = ALLOCATE_DEVICE_MEMORY(size_n)
 
 // Step 2: Transfer input data from Host to Device
 COPY_HOST_TO_DEVICE(pointer_A_Device, A, size_n)
 COPY_HOST_TO_DEVICE(pointer_B_Device, B, size_n)
 
 // Step 3: Configure kernel launch parameters
 threads_per_block = 256
 blocks_per_grid = CEILING(n / threads_per_block)
 
 // Step 4: Launch GPU kernel
 LAUNCH_KERNEL(vectorAdd, blocks_per_grid, threads_per_block, pointer_A_Device, pointer_B_Device, pointer_C_Device, n)
 
 // Step 5: Synchronize (wait for kernel completion)
 SYNCHRONIZE_DEVICE()
 
 // Step 6: Transfer results from Device to Host
 COPY_DEVICE_TO_HOST(C, pointer_C_Device, size_n)
 
 // Step 7: Free device memory
 FREE_DEVICE_MEMORY(pointer_A_Device)
 FREE_DEVICE_MEMORY(pointer_B_Device)
 FREE_DEVICE_MEMORY(pointer_C_Device)
END

ALGORITHM GPU_Kernel_vectorAdd
INPUT: Pointers to arrays A, B, C and size n
OUTPUT: None (modifies array C in place)

BEGIN
 // Calculate global thread ID
 thread_id = (block_index.x * block_dimension.x) + thread_index.x
 
 // Check if thread is within bounds
 IF thread_id < n THEN
 // Each thread performs one element operation
 C[thread_id] = A[thread_id] + B[thread_id]
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the complete CUDA workflow: 1) Memory allocation on GPU using cudaMalloc, 2) Data transfer from CPU to GPU using cudaMemcpy, 3) Kernel configuration with block and grid dimensions, 4) Kernel launch with triple angle bracket syntax, 5) Synchronization to ensure kernel completion, 6) Result retrieval from GPU to CPU, and 7) Memory cleanup. The kernel shows how each thread calculates its global index using blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x to independently process one element.

examples.title: Python: CUDA Vector Addition using PyCUDA
examples.description: Implements vector addition on GPU using PyCUDA library to demonstrate GPU computation
examples.code:
"""
GPU Vector Addition using PyCUDA
Demonstrates GPGPU computing with CUDA under Python
"""

import numpy as np
import pycuda.driver as cuda
import pycuda.autoinit
from pycuda.compiler import SourceModule

# Define the CUDA kernel in C
kernel_code = """
__global__ void vector_add(float *A, float *B, float *C, int n) {
 int idx = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;
 if (idx < n) {
 C[idx] = A[idx] + B[idx];
 }
}
"""

def main():
 # Define vector size
 n = 1024 * 1024 # 1 million elements
 print(f"Vector size: {n} elements")
 
 # Allocate host memory and initialize with random data
 A_host = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
 B_host = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
 C_host = np.zeros(n, dtype=np.float32)
 
 # Allocate device memory
 A_device = cuda.mem_alloc(A_host.nbytes)
 B_device = cuda.mem_alloc(B_host.nbytes)
 C_device = cuda.mem_alloc(C_host.nbytes)
 
 # Copy input data from host to device
 cuda.memcpy_htod(A_device, A_host)
 cuda.memcpy_htod(B_device, B_host)
 
 # Compile the kernel
 module = SourceModule(kernel_code)
 vector_add = module.get_function("vector_add")
 
 # Configure launch parameters
 threads_per_block = 256
 blocks_per_grid = (n + threads_per_block - 1) // threads_per_block
 
 # Launch the kernel
 vector_add(A_device, B_device, C_device, np.int32(n),
 block=(threads_per_block, 1, 1),
 grid=(blocks_per_grid, 1))
 
 # Copy result from device to host
 cuda.memcpy_dtoh(C_host, C_device)
 
 # Verify the result
 expected = A_host + B_host
 if np.allclose(C_host, expected, rtol=1e-5):
 print("SUCCESS: GPU computation verified!")
 else:
 print("ERROR: Results do not match!")
 
 # Cleanup is automatic when device objects go out of scope
 print(f"Computation used {blocks_per_grid} blocks with {threads_per_block} threads each")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation uses PyCUDA to interface with NVIDIA GPUs. The code defines a CUDA kernel in C as a string, compiles it at runtime, allocates device memory, transfers data, launches the kernel with appropriate block and grid dimensions, and retrieves results. The kernel calculates each thread's global index using blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x and each thread independently adds one element. This demonstrates the SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) execution model where thousands of threads run the same kernel code on different data elements.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - linear in number of elements
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - for input and output arrays on both host and device

examples.title: C: CUDA Vector Addition Implementation
examples.description: Implements vector addition using NVIDIA CUDA C to demonstrate basic GPGPU programming
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the complete CUDA programming workflow. Key elements: 1) __global__ qualifier marks the function as a GPU kernel, 2) Each thread calculates its global index using blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x, 3) cudaMalloc allocates GPU memory, 4) cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyHostToDevice transfers input data, 5) Triple angle bracket syntax (<<< >>>) launches the kernel with block and grid dimensions, 6) cudaDeviceSynchronize ensures the kernel completes before proceeding, 7) cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost retrieves results, and 8) cudaFree releases GPU memory. The boundary check 'if (i < n)' handles cases where vector size isn't evenly divisible by block size.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - each of n elements processed once by one thread
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - three float arrays on both host and device

examples.title: Algorithm: SIMT Execution Model and Warp Scheduling
examples.description: Explains the Single Instruction Multiple Threads model and how GPUs schedule warps for execution
examples.explanation: This pseudocode explains the SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) execution model fundamental to GPU computing. Key concepts: 1) Warps are groups of 32 threads that execute in lockstep on a Streaming Multiprocessor, 2) All threads in a warp execute the same instruction simultaneously on different data, 3) Warp divergence occurs when threads take different code paths (e.g., different branches in an if-else), degrading performance, 4) Memory coalescing occurs when consecutive threads access consecutive memory addresses, enabling efficient memory transactions, 5) The global thread ID formula blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x uniquely identifies each thread in the grid. GPUs hide memory latency by rapidly switching between warps, keeping execution units busy.
